html:
IX

The last light of sunset lay in the west; and a sullen wrack of cloud was mounting into the windless sky when Lawford entered the country graveyard again by its dark weatherworn lychgate. The old stone church with its square tower stood amid trees, its eastern window faintly aglow with crimson and purple. He could hear a steady, rather nasal voice through its open lattices. But the stooping stones and the cypresses were out of sight of its porch. He would not be seen down there. He paused a moment, however; his hat was drawn down over his eyes; he was shivering. Far over the harvest fields showed a growing pallor in the sky. He would have the moon to go home by.

тАЬHome!тАЭтАФthese trees, this tongueless companionship, this heavy winelike air, this soundless turfтАФthese in some obscure desolate fashion seemed far rather really home. His eyes wandered towards the fading crimson. And with that on his right hand he began softly, almost on tiptoe, descending the hill. It seemed to him that the steady eyes of the dead were watching him in his slow progress. The air was echoing with little faint, clear calls. He turned and snapped his fingers at a robin that was stalking him with its stony twittering from bush to bush.

But when after some little time he actually came out of the narrow avenue and looked down, his heart misgave him, for some one was already sitting there on his low and solitary seat beneath the cypresses. He stood hesitating, gazing steadily and yet half vacantly at the motionless figure, and in a while a face was lifted in his direction, and undisconcerted eyes calmly surveyed him.

тАЬI am afraid,тАЭ called Lawford rather nervouslyтБатАФтАЬI hope I am not intruding?тАЭ

тАЬNot at all, not at all,тАЭ said the stranger. тАЬI have no privileges here; at least as yet.тАЭ

Lawford again hesitated, then slowly advanced. тАЬItтАЩs astonishingly quiet and beautiful,тАЭ he said.

The stranger turned his head to glance over the fields. тАЬYes, it is, very,тАЭ he replied. There was the faintest accent, a little drawl of unfriendliness in the remark.

тАЬYou often sit here?тАЭ Lawford persisted.

The stranger raised his eyebrows. тАЬOh yes, often.тАЭ He smiled. тАЬIt is my own modest fashion of attending divine service. The congregation is rapt.тАЭ

тАЬMy visits,тАЭ said Lawford, тАЬhave been very fewтБатАФin fact, so far as I know, I have only once been here before.тАЭ

тАЬI envy you the novelty.тАЭ There was again the same faint unmistakable antagonism in voice and attitude; and yet so deep was the relief in talking to a fellow creature who hadnтАЩt the least suspicion of anything unusual in his appearance that Lawford was extremely disinclined to turn back. He made another effortтБатАФfor conversation with strangers had always been a difficulty to himтБатАФand advanced towards the seat. тАЬYou mustnтАЩt please let me intrude upon you,тАЭ he said, тАЬbut really I am very interested in this queer old place. Perhaps you would tell me something of its history?тАЭ He sat down. His companion moved slowly to the other side of the broken gravestone.

тАЬTo tell you the truth,тАЭ he replied, picking his way as it were from word to word, тАЬits тАШhistory,тАЩ as people call it, does not interest me in the least. After all, itтАЩs not when a thing is, but what it is, that much matters. What this isтАЩтБатАФhe glanced, with head bent, across the shadowy stones, тАЬis pretty evident. Of course, age has its charms.тАЭ

тАЬAnd is this very old?тАЭ

тАЬOh yes, itтАЩs old right enough, as things go; but even age, perhaps, is mainly an affair of the imagination. ThereтАЩs a tombstone near that little old hawthorn, and there are two others side by side under the wall, still even legibly late seventeenth century. ThatтАЩs pretty good weathering.тАЭ He smiled faintly. тАЬOf course, the church itself is centuries older, drenched with age. But sheтАЩs still sleepwalking while these old tombstones dream. Glowworms and crickets are not such bad bedfellows.тАЭ

тАЬWhat interested me most, I think,тАЭ said Lawford haltingly, тАЬwas this.тАЭ He pointed with his stick to the grave at his feet.

тАЬAh, yes, SabathierтАЩs,тАЭ said the stranger; тАЬI know his peculiar history almost by heart.тАЭ

Lawford found himself staring with unusual concentration into the rather long and pale face. тАЬNot, I suppose,тАЭ he resumed faintlyтБатАФтАЬnot, I suppose, beyond whatтАЩs there.тАЭ

His companion leant his hand on the old stooping tombstone. тАЬWell, you know, thereтАЩs a good deal thereтАЭтБатАФhe stooped overтБатАФтАЬif you read between the lines. Even if you donтАЩt.тАЭ

тАЬA suicide,тАЭ said Lawford, under his breath.

тАЬYes, a suicide; thatтАЩs why our Christian countrymen have buried him outside of the fold. Dead or alive, they try to keep the wolf out.тАЭ

тАЬIs this, then, unconsecrated ground?тАЭ said Lawford.

тАЬHavenтАЩt you noticed,тАЭ drawled the other, тАЬhow green the grass grows down here, and how very sharp are poor old SabathierтАЩs thorns? Besides, he was a stranger, and theyтБатАФkept him out.тАЭ

тАЬBut, surely,тАЭ said Lawford, тАЬwas it so entirely a matter of choiceтБатАФthe laws of the Church? If he did kill himself, he did.тАЭ

The stranger turned with a little shrug. тАЬI donтАЩt suppose itтАЩs a matter of much consequence to him. I fancied I was his only friend. May I venture to ask why you are interested in the poor old thing?тАЭ

LawfordтАЩs mind was as calm and shallow as a millpond. тАЬOh, a rather unusual thing happened to me here,тАЭ he said. тАЬYou say you often come?тАЭ

тАЬOften,тАЭ said the stranger rather curtly.

тАЬHas anythingтБатАФeverтБатАФoccurred?тАЭ

тАЬтАКтАШOccurred?тАЩтАКтАЭ He raised his eyebrows. тАЬI wish it had. I come here simply, as I have said, because itтАЩs quiet; because I prefer the company of those who never answer me back, and who do not so much as condescend to pay me the least attention.тАЭ He smiled and turned his face towards the quiet fields.

Lawford, after a long pause, lifted his eyes. тАЬDo you think,тАЭ he said softly, тАЬit is possible one ever could?тАЭ

тАЬтАКтАШOne ever could?тАЩтАКтАЭ

тАЬAnswer back?тАЭ

There was a low rotting wall of stone encompassing SabathierтАЩs grave; on this the stranger sat down. He glanced up rather curiously at his companion. тАЬSeldom the time and the place and the revenant altogether. The thought has occurred to others,тАЭ he ventured to add.

тАЬOf course, of course,тАЭ said Lawford eagerly. тАЬBut it is an absolutely new one to me. I donтАЩt mean that I have never had such an idea, just in oneтАЩs own superficial way; butтАЭтБатАФhe paused and glanced swiftly into the fast-thickening twilightтБатАФтАЬI wonder: are they, do you think, really, all quite dead?тАЭ

тАЬCall and see!тАЭ taunted the stranger softly.

тАЬAh, yes, I know,тАЭ said Lawford. тАЬBut I believe in the resurrection of the body; that is what we say; and supposing, when a man diesтБатАФsupposing it was most frightfully against oneтАЩs will; that one hated the awful inaction that death brings, shutting a poor devil up like a child kicking against the door in a dark cupboard; one mightтБатАФsurely one mightтБатАФjust quietly, you know, try to get out? WouldnтАЩt you?тАЭ he added.

тАЬAnd, surely,тАЭ he found himself beginning gently to argue again, тАЬsurely, what about, say, him?тАЭ He nodded towards the old and broken grave that lay between them.

тАЬWhat, Sabathier?тАЭ the other echoed, laying his hand upon the stone.

And a sheer enormous abyss of silence seemed to follow the unanswerable question.

тАЬHe was a stranger; it says so. Good God!тАЭ said Lawford, тАЬhow he must have wanted to get home! He killed himself, poor wretch, think of the fret and fever he must have been inтБатАФjust before. Imagine it.тАЭ

тАЬBut it might, you know,тАЭ suggested the other with a smileтБатАФтАЬmight have been sheer indifference.тАЭ

тАЬтАКтАШNicholas Sabathier, Stranger to this parishтАЩтБатАФno, no,тАЭ said Lawford, his heart beating as if it would choke him, тАЬI donтАЩt fancy it was indifference.тАЭ

It was almost too dark now to distinguish the strangerтАЩs features but there seemed a faint suggestion of irony in his voice. тАЬAnd how do you suppose your angry naughty child would set about it? ItтАЩs narrow quarters; how would he begin?тАЭ

Lawford sat quite still. тАЬYou sayтБатАФI hope I am not detaining youтБатАФyou say you have come here, sat here often, on this very seat; have you ever hadтБатАФhave you ever fallen asleep here?тАЭ

тАЬWhy do you ask?тАЭ inquired the other curiously.

тАЬI was only wondering,тАЭ said Lawford. He was cold and shivering. He felt instinctively it was madness to sit on here in the thin gliding mist that had gathered in swathes above the grass, milk-pale in the rising moon. The stranger turned away from him.

тАЬтАКтАШFor in that sleep of death what dreams may come must give us pause,тАЩтАКтАЭ he said slowly, with a little satirical catch on the last word. тАЬWhat did you dream?тАЭ

Lawford glanced helplessly about him. The moon cast lean grey beams of light between the cypresses. But to his wide and wandering eyes it seemed that a radiance other than hers haunted these mounds and leaning stones. тАЬHave you ever noticed it?тАЭ he said, putting out his hand towards his unknown companion; тАЬthis stone is cracked from head to foot?тБатАКтБатАж But thereтАЭтБатАФhe rose stiff and chilledтБатАФтАЬI am afraid I have bored you with my company. You came here for solitude, and I have been trying to convince you that we are surrounded with witnesses. You will forgive my intrusion?тАЭ There was a kind of old-fashioned courtesy in his manner that he himself was dimly aware of. He held out his hand.

тАЬI hope you will think nothing of the kind,тАЭ said the other earnestly; тАЬhow could it be in any sense an intrusion? ItтАЩs the old story of Bluebeard. And I confess I too should very much like a peep into his cupboard. Who wouldnтАЩt? But there, itтАЩs merely a matter of time, I suppose.тАЭ He paused, and together they slowly ascended the path already glimmering with a heavy dew. At the porch they paused once more. And now it was the stranger that held out his hand.

тАЬPerhaps,тАЭ he said, тАЬyou will give me the pleasure of some day continuing our talk. As for our friend below, it so happens that I have managed to pick up a little more of his history than the sexton seems to have heard ofтБатАФif you would care some time or other to share it. I live only at the foot of the hill, not half a mile distant. Perhaps you could spare the time now?тАЭ

Lawford took out his watch, тАЬYou are really very kind,тАЭ he said. тАЬBut, perhapsтБатАФwell, whatever that history may be, I think you would agree that mine is evenтБатАФbut, there, IтАЩve talked too much about myself already. Perhaps tomorrow?тАЭ

тАЬWhy, tomorrow, then,тАЭ said his companion. тАЬItтАЩs a flat wooden house, on the left-hand side. Come at any time of the eveningтАЭ; he paused again and smiledтБатАФтАЬthe third house after the Rectory, which is marked up on the gate. My name is HerbertтБатАФHerbert Herbert to be precise.тАЭ

Lawford took out his pocketbook and a card. тАЬMine,тАЭ he said, handing it gravely to his companion, тАЬis LawfordтБатАФat leastтБатАКтБатАжтАЭ It was really the first time that either had seen the otherтАЩs face at close quarters and clear-lit; and on LawfordтАЩs a moon almost at the full shone dazzlingly. He saw an expressionтБатАФdismay, incredulity, overwhelming astonishmentтБатАФstart suddenly into the dark, rather indifferent eyes.

тАЬWhat is it?тАЭ he cried, hastily stooping close.

тАЬWhy,тАЭ said the other, laughing and turning away, тАЬI think the moon must have bewitched me too.тАЭ
